chunk_summary: This section outlines measurable indicators for monitoring and improving the safety and delivery of enteral and parenteral nutrition in resource-variable settings, with specific guidelines for energy, protein, and safety metrics in pediatric critical care and ward settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of tracking delivered versus prescribed energy and protein in enteral and parenteral nutrition.
Identify safety indicators such as aspiration-related harm, biochemical events, and central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSI).
Learn practical methods for monitoring growth and addressing delivery errors in pediatric patients.
Incorporate feasibility considerations for nutrition delivery in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Aim to deliver at least two-thirds of the prescribed energy and protein target by the end of the first week in the PICU or earlier on the ward.
Trigger escalation if delivery falls below 80% for 24–48 hours.
Track aspiration-related harm per 100 tube-days, including tube dislodgement and emesis with desaturation.
Monitor biochemical safety events such as hypoglycemia, hypophosphatemia, and triglyceride excursions during feed or PN advances.
Track CLABSI episodes in PN patients, including line-days, dressing integrity, and time-to-culture and antibiotics.
Include growth metrics like daily weight in critical illness and weekly length/height and MUAC for longer stays.
reference_quote: Operational quality improves when nutrition is monitored like a vital sign. A minimal dashboard should tie each metric to an action.
